scenario_results.reason: The lane advanced the request through reservation, check-out, review, return, and closure only when location, custody, and return evidence remained explicit.
Missing style, sample-type, and due-date facts kept the request blocked and produced a bounded intake exception instead of an unsupported pull.
The missing sample was treated as a traceability blocker and escalated with conflicting location evidence rather than being guessed into a status.
Conflicting requests for the same sample preserved custody discipline and forced use of tenant priority rules or explicit escalation.
Conflicting PLM, sample-tracker, and courier records blocked progression until authoritative reconciliation existed.
A damaged or safety-significant sample moved into hold posture and routed to quality or compliance without implied release authority.
Supplier and material substitution pressure was rejected cleanly and routed to sourcing and supplier-quality owners.
Technical signoff work was refused and the lane stayed narrow to sample-room evidence and custody facts.
Low-confidence handling explicitly requested the missing tenant policy, priority, and system-of-record facts rather than improvising.
Conflicting policy about using held or recalled samples was escalated instead of being merged into an unsupported release answer.
Tenant terminology was adapted without losing baseline style identity, custody, hold, and return controls.
Final care-label and compliance approval ownership was rejected and routed to the correct adjacent owner with evidence support.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to apparel sample-room execution, custody control, traceability, and bounded escalation.
Functionality coverage includes all shared abilities, all execution-processing-and-fulfillment abilities, and all manufacturing-and-supply-chain abilities required by the audit contract.
